markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Zugero](/Deities.aspx?ID=509)
(The Dream Battler)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 312

Zugero encompasses both ends of the spectrum of battle. When she was a mortal, she was revered as a fantastic general; no other [goblin](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=62) had been said to emerge victorious in so many raids. Zugero considered there to be no need to enter a battle if there was no guaranteed victory. She planned out ambush points, having others create traps or use the environment to their favor. She kept her brilliant tactical mind sharp with constant rest. It wasn’t uncommon for her soldiers to find her napping or drinking midengagement, nestled away safely behind back lines. According to tales, it was her most powerful nap ever that eventually elevated her to become the Dream Battler. She now appears as a goblin with pale green skin clad in slapdash armor made from scraps, carrying a number of armaments and trap devices around her belt. Depending on the observer, she either appears alert for battle or on the verge of falling asleep, which many goblins state is a sign as to which task the observer should focus on at the time.

To force her followers to rest and relax, Zugero whispers strategies or gives epiphanies on how to face a battle or ambush only when her followers are ignoring their current duties. To Zugero, a well-rested mind is vital to achieving victory in battle, while also being a fantastic reward for a successful confrontation. She is selective; not everyone can gain her advice, and she is claimed to judge her followers by the number of scars they possess. Experience makes a cunning and great tactician, and that is gained through battle, or failing at deceiving the enemy into doing what one wants. Thus, those who want to be one of Zugero’s chosen need to face danger, and think strategically how to survive it.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Goblin Gods](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=27)

**Edicts** Fight today so you can rest tomorrow, work smart instead of hard, slack off and get away with it

**Anathema** Launch yourself thoughtlessly into work or combat

**Area of Concern**
Ambushes, lurking, slacking

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Grundinnar](/Deities.aspx?ID=514)
(The Peacemaker)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 312

When Torag was forging Grundinnar, he graced him with a tongue of silver, and a heart of gold and iron. When [Folgrit](/Deities.aspx?ID=87) saw this gift and breathed life into him, she asked that Grundinnar be kind and loving always. He took this as both mandate and life goal, and now works to bring peace and familial connection to all who worship him and their allies. It's said that Grundinnar practiced quelling conflict by saving his sister [Bolka](/Deities.aspx?ID=85) from her many suitors, and in doing so learned to forge peace, but this is only half-truth. He truly learned to be a peacemaker by dealing with his bickering family, for even the gods among dwarves can be quarrelsome and taciturn. 

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Dead or Missing Deities](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=34)

**Anathema** Sow discord among friends and allies, attack during parley

**Area of Concern**
Alliance, family, friendship, truth

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Sturovenen](/Deities.aspx?ID=523)
(The Dragoneagle)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Sturovenen was born to the second generation of spirit guides, the mighty son of [Dragon](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=395) and [Eagle](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=406). Noble, kind, and a leader by example, Sturovenen’s worship spread quickly and he ascended to full godhood in astonishingly short order. Sturovenen had two great loves: the mortal man Chakli, who became Sturovenen’s first god caller, and the spirit guide Canary. 

Sturovenen and Chakli founded the Neverhome Clan together and made it a refuge for those with no clan of their own and nowhere else to seek shelter. Though Chakli’s life was short even by mortal standards, he lived to choose an heir who became Sturovenen’s next god caller and that tradition carried on generation after generation. It became a teaching among Sturovenen’s god callers that they had a responsibility to the Dragoneagle to ensure that he never lacked a mortal companion to preach wisdom and patience, for Sturovenen’s selflessness was well-known and an ancient prophecy spoken by the spirit guide Fox foretold that the very traits which made the Dragoneagle so well-loved would one day be his undoing. The god callers shared the duty of looking out for Sturovenen with his dawn-feathered children, the offspring of Sturovenen and Canary, many of whom took on god callers of their own and became quite famous among the people of Sarkoris outside of their father’s great shadow.

Sadly, Fox’s foretelling proved correct when the Godsrain struck Sarkoris. With the walls between planes already thin and damaged, [demonic](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=387) hordes sought to reopen the Worldwound and Sturovenen, accompanied by his last god caller and his many dawn-feathered children, flew to the nation’s defense. Sturovenen ultimately gave his life, snatching a corrupted warshard from demonic claws and purifying it in a rainbow explosion that also seems to have erased his own existence.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Bifrons](/Deities.aspx?ID=525)
(The Second Fate)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

A dogmatic obsession with fate, luck, and patterns paints Bifrons as a questionable partner. They experience the highest highs and the lowest lows, celebrating each equally. Distinguishing whether windfall and forfeiture are brought by chance or inevitability is pointless to their mind, for it is all within the parameters of the universe’s game. Their base is built on the teasing of mortals against the odds; if they lose, such only proves the pattern, while if they win, fate smiled upon them and they should thank Bifrons for their encouragement. Patrons of the Second Fate consider misfortune as an investment in greater rewards to come, gambling they’ll be free to collect the consequences. Many promise to contract with the [devil](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389) only temporarily, just until they’ve had enough. Few are able to satisfy this conviction, but Bifrons loves an underdog. Bifrons appears as a gambling devil, a devil made entirely of gold, clad in royal regalia with a face that appears to be either shouting in glee or screaming in agony.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Furcas](/Deities.aspx?ID=526)
(The Knight of Laurels)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Orange poppies arranged in a flame on a green field grace the Knight of Laurels’s standard. His laurels and wreaths are a heavy collection of favors asked and given by the cherished of each court defeated on his decennial campaign across the plane. His appearance is that of an enormous centaur covered in dark armor made of hardened, thorny vines. His immortal host, champions who damned their souls in the name of obligation and who know the true meaning of sacrifice, ride to the end of days in the Hanging Marches. Knights who will win at any cost, foresters who will burn the copse and all its life within to nourish regrowth, and physicians who will administer medicine that has equal chance of killing: those who will pay the highest prices salute Furcas. 

If given the commission to ride out from the planes of [Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=42), no throne would go unmarked by the horseshoes of Furcas’s cavalry; scores live in blissful ignorance of their luck, that the infernal duke is instead charged to guard the hellmouth Voulgaz. It is said if one challenges and defeats a knight of Furcas in single combat, their price is a panacea mortared by the Knight of Laurels, a remedy that would heal the body of any wound and the soul of any languish.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Haborym](/Deities.aspx?ID=527)
(The Burning Heath)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Not seen outside their bastille of hellfire-stoked iron since their designation as a duke, Haborym keeps vigil over the most dangerous beings in the planes. Haborym’s 8-mile-long prison is a testament to their dedication, a crucible for the souls to be reforged anew. Any interned within have the option to challenge the governor to a single game of their choosing: early discharge for victory, servitude for defeat. To Haborym’s dismay, not one prisoner has exercised this right in generations. Depictions show the Burning Hearth as a four-armed humanoid lacking a mouth and with skin that appears to have been burned to near ashes. 

Those who serve their sentence all have volunteered to join the Panopticon’s watch, guarding over their former fellow inmates. Lawkeeps who catch, judges who charge, and smiths who forge the bonds of captivity, who believe through continual breaking and reforging the weak are honed strong and true, these are the ilk of Haborym. “It is for their own good” is their guiding axiom.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Infernal Dukes](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=28)

**Edicts** Identify all exits, take your enemies alive, enact malicious compliance

**Anathema** Deny clemency without offering an opportunity for appeal, wear chains or bracelets on your person, misplace anything within your charge

**Area of Concern**
Immolation, incarceration, renewal

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Malthus](/Deities.aspx?ID=528)
(The Five-Beaked)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Perched atop the Watch of Arocard, Malthus surveys his grand works of architecture, expansion, and waste. His form is that of an ashen man with the torso of an ibis and five insidious beaks. He observes, unmoving, for such long spans that regolith accumulates upon his crest—a crust that crumbles off like shale as he takes flight. Since mortals first piled earth to make walls, Malthus has manipulated them to his coordination. From his five beaks, he promises favor for small, subtle design modifications that in their singular instance are innocuous. From up above, the buildings across continents and built over generations begin to make greater patterns of infernal geometry. Countless sigils waiting to be exploited are hidden in plain sight, their purpose known only to the Five-Beaked above and below.

Those who would chase the very edge of reality in search of a beauty that only they understand, who seek coexistence of form and function, who would die unknown in their age to be remembered as a genius in the following, are cohorts to the Five-Beaked.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Infernal Dukes](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=28)

**Edicts** Scan your surroundings from above, join form with function, waste as an act of luxury

**Anathema** Take impulsive action, demolish buildings, take in moderation

Architecture, expansion, waste

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Nergal](/Deities.aspx?ID=529)
(The Slow Death)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Preceded by the stench of sun-ripened corpses, one is never caught unawares by Nergal. The sights of the battlefield, driving many mortals to teary eyes and corkscrewed mouths holding back retches, stir him with giddy laughter from his many orifices. Classically, the Slow Death has invested in the ways and means of war, nurturing death on a nation-state scale. Bodies of the dead were left on the ground, but also walking back to their homes to spread invisible death. Recently, Nergal and his sycophants have taken an interest in the sciences. Sowing doubt and misinformation among physicians and patients alike, they wrench rifts ever wider between those who would champion against infection and rot. 

For now, Nergal is weaker than their counterpart [Nurgal](/Deities.aspx?ID=78), but no less lethal. Cunning out of necessity rather than design and unwilling to concede the wager with his twin, Nergal’s armies are at war inside all who breathe. It only takes one mortal to lose their battle with the plague, before entire populations are brought to their knees before the Slow Death. As if some kind of reflection of his [demon lord](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=5) counterpart, Nergal appears as a maned [draconic](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=395) being with the torso of a humanoid man and the legs of a [lion](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2866).

<column gap="tiny">

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Titivilus](/Deities.aspx?ID=530)
(The Scrivener)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Upon the backs of all beholden to him, Titivilus scratches the record of [Hell](/Planes.aspx?ID=42) with long claws. His is a slender form, almost like a [human](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=64) man stretched well beyond his typical proportions, with skin the color of the deep purples of a sunset. Nine hands, each with nine fingers, transcribe every word spoken within the rings in illustrious longhand and great detail, down to the tones and rhythms. Steel is a crude tool for change; the Scrivening Count would crumble empires with but a whisper. A spear and shield may break a body, but lies, propaganda, rhetoric, and truth manipulate the hearts and minds that drive the hands.

Politicians, barristers, poets, and the like count on Titivilus for guidance on how to manipulate the masses. In exchange, they give stories and rarefied texts. With these, Titivilus has composed the most comprehensive and unbiased history of each plane from the beginning of time to ever exist.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Infernal Dukes](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=28)

**Edicts** Quote people back to themselves, chronicle the times you live in, skew the truth to your purpose

**Anathema** Allow a rumor to go unchallenged, destroy records, trust in memory alone

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Ussharassim](/Deities.aspx?ID=531)
(The Spurring Scar)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

From a bacchanal gallery of gloried names remembered fondly, Ussharassim bids all who crave the warm embrace of fame, heroism, and renown to join the revelry. His kind are the young farmers who look over the glade dreaming of bards telling how they vanquished a great foe, the fiddlers who pray someone will play their song in a hundred years’ time, the adventurer who wished everyone knew their name and smiled. Hopes for grand adventure are met with the demise of their loved ones, spurning on their campaign of revenge. Poets are gifted heartbreaks that rattle their core and inspire refrains that will be quoted for generations, the glory and honor of which are indebted to the Spurring Scar forever. From his table, surrounded by heroes, villains, and masters of their time, he shares tales of all who would be the next greats, his voice crystal clear from behind a mirror mask covering a scarred visage.

<column gap="tiny">

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Vapula](/Deities.aspx?ID=532)
(The Exchequer)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

With a greed that rivals that of the oldest [dragons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=395), Vapula relishes knowledge. He particularly savors the moment when one learns something new and has to recontextualize everything as their reality changes. In the chase for revelation, he has acquired quite the collection and developed a knack for cataloging and security. All that is lost is found in his vaults. The prayers of the widower who lost his ring, the pup whose human forgot them, the knight whose sword was knocked into the crevasse in their most dire hour—their quiet offers cross the Keeper of the Pyrite Vault’s ears before any others. For easy favors, wishes can come true. [Devils](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389), [demons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=387), archmagi, and scores of [angels](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=359) come to account with Vapula, each knowing any treasures or threats they deposit are safe and accounted for. Threats from [Asmodeus](/Deities.aspx?ID=278) himself could not ply Vapula, for the Exchequer’s word is his bond, and he is all the more respected for it. Those that meet with Vapula encounter a living stone statue of a horned man wearing an elegant robe, his visage changing every time one looks away.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Infernal Dukes](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=28)

**Edicts** Finders keepers, speak with others in their native language, ask more questions than you give answers

**Anathema** Work in approximations, renegotiate, fail to uphold your word

Discovery, hubris, scholars

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Atropos](/Deities.aspx?ID=533)
(The Judge of Judges)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Atropos, known among the [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) ushers as the Judge of Judges and the Last Sister, is [Pharasma’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) youngest (and perhaps only) daughter, ruling over the Boneyard alongside her mother as the penultimate arbiter of mortal fates. The psychopomp manifests as a large [nosoi](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3147) with a shimmering, silver mask and a long tail of vivid peacock feathers, each bearing a living mortal eye: one feather with one eye for every soul past, present, and future to receive her judgment. When appearing in mortal guise, Atropos instead takes the form of a young [human](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=64) girl, and she often favors this form during appearances in her mother’s court. 

The psychopomps whisper that Pharasma is training Atropos to be her successor, and the nosoi will inherit the rule of the Boneyard and the role of final judge of mortal souls in the times beyond the end, when this universe has died and is reborn anew. Until the day of the end comes, Atropos presides over mortal sleep instead of true death, and she cares for the souls of those who die as children, safeguarding their afterlives. She is worshipped by some nosoi, and her mortal cults include soothsayers and those with troubled sleep and chronic illnesses.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Barzahk](/Deities.aspx?ID=534)
(The Passage)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Appearing as an enormous migratory bird—usually a corvid, but sometimes a songbird—draped in robes and carrying a tombstone lock and a giant bone key, Barzahk the Passage is the [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher who maintains the Dead Roads, the secret back routes between the planes and the mortal world. Among mortals, they are worshipped as a patron of compasses, travelers, and vigils. Barzahk is tasked with transporting the souls of those who die far from home to ensure that they reach their proper destination. Unfortunately, Barzahk wanders far and wide, and so they rarely attend to this duty. Thus Barzahk’s followers, both psychopomps and mortals, take it upon themselves to care for lost souls, both literal and figurative. Like their patron, followers of Barzahk are often migratory, helping those they find along the way.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Ceyannan](/Deities.aspx?ID=535)
(The Shepherd)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Within every mortal heart is a rejection of death, an untamable desire to fight and live, and from this desperate impulse, Ceyannan the Shepherd was born. The usher rose from the troubled currents of the River of Souls to gather and guide the souls of the fallen into the afterlife. Fragments of the Shepherd remain in the hearts of all mortals, and for each soul guided across the river a missing piece returns to Ceyannan, bringing them closer to wholeness. They abhor when souls are displaced from the cycle, and whenever great, existential threats emerge against the cosmos, the usher of lost souls is there to contain them. 

Ceyannan travels along the River of Souls and through the Universe, using their connection to their missing pieces to gather their flock and bring wayward souls to rest in the Boneyard. Their mortal cult attracts worshippers who search for something lost or elusive, be they bounty hunters pursuing escaped fugitives or those with lives marked by deep losses of self. On the day the last mortal dies and Ceyannan is made whole, the [psychopomps](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) believe the Shepherd will embrace their own final rest as well, passing away from this world alongside mortality.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Dammar](/Deities.aspx?ID=536)
(The Denied)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

In the Age of Creation, the first time a soul was plucked from the River of Souls and returned to life, a [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher of life and resurrection emerged. Dammar the Denied is that first piece of resurrection magic made manifest, the psychopomp usher of life and journeys back from the Boneyard, and they ensure mortals don’t make that journey too often or too easily. Alongside souls returning from the grave, Dammar presides over medicine, doctors who stave off death before it even arrives, alcohol (particularly, though not exclusively, used in medical preparations), and the unyielding tenacity of cockroaches and other insects who look upon death and refuse. 

On [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), Dammar is worshipped by gamblers, physicians, brewers, and vintners, and by those rare mortals who defy the laws of death. Boneyard lore holds that the Denied created hangovers for mortals so they could look beyond the veil of life and death. Their preferred form is stout and androgynous, face and body shrouded behind layers of opulent silks, with six insectile legs; they manifest arms only as they are needed, sometimes dozens at a time.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Dramindyr](/Deities.aspx?ID=537)
(The Skin of Creation)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

[The Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26) as it currently exists depends on strict boundaries, the separation of reality into distinct matter instead of primordial chaos. The planes are likewise divided from each other by metaphysical borders, pierced by only portals and planar rifts. Dramindyr, the Skin of Creation, is the first arbitrator who forms these perimeters; their very body is the membrane that holds every plant, animal, mineral, and dimension separate from one another. To cross between planes is to walk along Dramindyr’s spine, and to rip holes in the planar fabric is to pierce Dramindyr’s infinite coils. Indeed, the presence of the Worldwound on Golarion was an open sore on Dramindyr’s side for over a century, and its closure may have caused Dramindyr to return more of their attention to their rare adherents upon Golarion. 

As most mortals maintain their own physical integrity simply by existing—outside of an extremely messy death—Dramindyr mostly tasks mortals with maintaining planar boundaries. The encroachment of planes upon other planes is to be discouraged outside of sanctioned portals, a task in which many other divinities aid Dramindyr. Dramindyr also appears to frown upon the use of summoning magic to draw creatures from other planes; the arbitrator is unlikely to act against a singular being connected to a spellcaster, such as a familiar or eidolon, but strongly opposes the likes of genie binders or [devil](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389) summoners. This position is much less supported by people among the Inner Sea, though Dramindyr’s worshippers are rare enough that this has never risen to an official political issue.

The most extreme agents of chaos, such as [protean](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457) lords or certain [fiends](/Traits.aspx?ID=600), occasionally make attempts to destroy Dramindyr to weaken their rigid control over individuals and matter. While it is unlikely that Dramindyr’s destruction would cause the complete collapse of reality into seething chaos, such an event would likely be apocalyptic in its scope. For this reason, Dramindyr has no shortage of defenders, as even the most wicked of devils has little desire to be rendered into primordial soup.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Il’Surrish](/Deities.aspx?ID=538)
(The Wanderer)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

Resembling nothing so much as the swirling, uncontrolled cacophony of planar potential that is the [Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43) itself, Il’surrish the Wanderer is a formless, shapeless [protean](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457) lord presiding over the untold infinities of unrealized potentials and possibilities. It celebrates the undefined and undecided, plans that linger unmade, power and discipline relinquished, and wild, unrestrained transformation. The Wanderer defies the pattern of chaotic philosophies championing the extremes of personal liberties and independence, teaching its followers to yield their avidity for control and instead allow the chaos inherent to the multiverse to guide them like a leaf gliding through a stream. 

Il’Surrish’s mortal cult attracts artists, martyrs, and transmuters. Its protean clergy in the Maelstrom, known as the Asomatous Clutch, have the same insubstantiality as their patron. These groups rarely erect permanent temples to Il’Surrish, instead performing their rites and ceremonies along river rapids, at wells of anarchic planar energy, and anywhere the Maelstrom touches another plane.

The Wanderer’s followers typically use a swirling, cosmic orb to represent the formless Protean Lord. Though it sometimes manifests as a slithering protean of intense cerulean light, it prefers to possess [demon](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=387), [devil](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=389), or [archon](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=365) vessels to puppet and speak through.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Inna](/Deities.aspx?ID=539)
(The Death of Stars)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 314

The building blocks of the mortal [Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26) were forged within suns, each element in reality created within the burning furnace of their cores. Almost all life in the Universe still depends on these suns, with both animals and plants nourished by the life-giving energy of these blazing spheres. Yet these gifts are granted by the slow death of the stars, their eons-long immolation finally ending in an explosive nova and a collapse into a black hole. Inna, the Death of Stars, is the poison that lurks in the heart of these doomed suns, the slow murder of these celestial bodies that facilitates all other life. This gift does not come without a price: the death throes of these cosmic giants offer an invisible, radioactive death to those who come too close.

Few are both powerful enough or knowledgeable enough to attract Inna’s attention, and most who do succumb to radiation or explosive accidents. Adherents of Inna tend to accept this exchange, sacrificing their bodies for knowledge and power. The powers of creation and destruction that Inna wields is unparalleled, and for those few who can grasp the concept of the first arbitrator in their minds, the bargain is worth it.

Inna is unusually responsive to prayer for an arbitrator, but this is counteracted by their radioactive influence, which often kills their worshippers. Followers of Inna are thus almost uniformly scholars, as most who rely on the sun in their day-to-day lives can find safer gods to follow. While Inna rarely makes demands or enacts punishment on mortals, they do sometimes send heralds: terrifyingly calm animals with tiny burning stars in place of eyes.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Monitor Demigods
](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

**Anathema** Douse ashes with water instead of letting them burn out, bury a body instead of cremating it, curse the sun

**Area of Concern**
Black holes, energy, radiation, stars

</column>

</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

<column gap="tiny">

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Liisglan](/Deities.aspx?ID=540)
(The Force Within)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

All things, regardless of what they are, have inherent rules that govern their shape, structure, how they react to outside forces, and so on. Liisglan maintains these rules, keeping them contained within their allowed parameters so as to maintain the orderly structure of existence as a whole. If a creature were to suddenly break these rules to become unaffected by gravity or unable to interact with solid objects without some kind of outside influence, it could lead to ultimate disaster. The rules are in place for a reason, and the Forces Within does not suffer any who try to break these rules.

Magic might appear to be something that goes against the exact laws of reality, but even magic has governing rules. The first arbitrator already accounted for the existence of magic in the consideration of maintaining order. Magic, no matter how powerful, is not eternal, and even enormous changes that feel effectively timeless will eventually reach their end. As such, a temporary inversion of gravity or a brief dance with intangibility via magic are permissible. It’s only when one attempts to push magic or reality to points where it is not meant to be that Liisglan acts.

Liisglan sees patterns and sequences in all things, and they push to maintain these whenever possible. The ordered way of things trends toward natural sequences. The existence of these patterns and sequences serves as examples of order remaining intact. Harmonious music is also full of patterns, which pleases Liisglan. They often bless anyone who maintains order with brief, pleasant melodies in the form of chime notes.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Monitor Demigods
](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

**Edicts** Uphold the laws of the physical and magical world, arrange things with orderly patterns or logic, make use of music during your work

**Anathema** break a rule of physics or magic, create discordant music, intentionally disrupt a non-detrimental pattern

**Area of Concern**
Governing physics, harmony, sequences

</column>

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Monad](/Deities.aspx?ID=541)
(The Condition of All)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

All [aeons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=357) come from, return to, are connected with, and are guided by the Monad. The Condition of All is not a deity in the traditional sense. It exists both within and outside [the multiverse](/Rules.aspx?ID=2988) and has influence over the entirety of existence. Though generally content to allow the multiverse to run its course, the Monad directs its aeons to intervene when events deviate from their ineffable design. Mortal scholars often personify the Monad as the deity of creation, the infinite, and truth. Even so, few worship it, and the Monad pays very little attention to mortal petitioners. Instead, scholars study and plot aeons’ actions, striving to discern the Monad’s ultimate goal or to uncover universal truths they can exploit for their own purposes. Only a rare few mortals can master the asceticism necessary to connect with the Monad, gaining hidden knowledge and powers akin to those of other divine spellcasters. While the Monad seems to be more than capable of maintaining the sanctity of existence, the fact that it broke off pieces of itself to create the first arbitrators suggests the Monad is either fully incapable of doing so alone or is choosing to focus its attention toward other matters.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Monitor Demigods
](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

**Edicts** Ensure balance between opposing forces, mediate disagreements

**Anathema** Allow your personal motivations to determine a major decision

Creation, the infinite, truth

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Mother Vulture](/Deities.aspx?ID=542)
(The Flesheater)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Though she usually appears as a blind young woman, in her natural form, the Flesheater is an enormous, bloated creature with four wings and two long necks topped with masked heads and gaping maws. Mother Vulture reflects the dualistic process of decay. She is a vicious killer whose feasts stain the ground, representing the death inherent in decay, but also a thoughtful mother, representing the new life that can take root in the fertile soil left in the wake of destruction. She is reviled by many, for she is the ugliness of reality on full display, but there are some who can see the beauty hidden within her stark pragmatism. Mother Vulture’s mortal worshippers, who often dwell in deserts and swamps, revere her as the patron of consumption, renewal, and transformation. In this last aspect, she judges those souls who sought redemption in life, deciding whether their atonement was sufficient to avoid an undesired afterlife.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Mrtyu](/Deities.aspx?ID=543)
(Death’s Consort)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Everyone who dies has a final thought, a last word, an emotion swollen in the heart, or a feeling caught in the throat. For Mrtyu, a mortal soldier, those last words were a reflection on one thing: love. This was a final thought that intrigued the [Lady of Graves](/Deities.aspx?ID=290), for she had never seen its like before. She wooed the fallen soldier in the afterlife, and in growing to understand him, she understood the passions of all who live. 

Mrtyu became Death’s Consort, the [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher of poetry, trauma, and war. He cares for the souls of soldiers, murder victims, those who die gripped by their strongest emotions, and those who take their own lives. When a soul is too tormented for rest, Mrtyu feels their pain in his own heart when they rise from their graves. His realm, the Garden Anima, is a peaceful refuge within Pharasma’s Court where those who suffer traumatic deaths can contemplate and work to understand their fates in safety.

Within [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), Mrtyu’s mortal cult is made up of soldiers, lovers, counselors, and single parents, and nearly all his faithful are poets of some description. Temples devoted to Death’s Consort can be found across battlefields and inside fortresses, gardens, and medical centers.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Narriseminek](/Deities.aspx?ID=544)
(The Crownless, the Maker of Kings)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Called the Crownless and the Maker of Kings, Narriseminek often appears as a [protean](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457) with a scar around their pate, as if left by a burning crown. Their true form, though similar, is rarely seen: an iridescent and golden protean with a halo of burning eyes but empty eye sockets. The protean lord of ascendance, [keketars](/Monsters.aspx?ID=3146), and revelations rarely interacts with nonproteans, but to their protean worshippers, they offer exalted transformations and revelations that can change a being’s entire outlook. Worshippers of Narriseminek, both mortal and protean, spend their time divining the future and using magic to transform their bodies—a practice they also extend to any other willing creatures that ask. Their revelations take the form of patterns emerging in otherwise random events; Narriseminek’s followers reject astrology and other forms of divination based on predictable cycles.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[The Pale Horse](/Deities.aspx?ID=545)
(The Lash and the Plough)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

The Pale Horse stalks the banks of the River of Souls. This has been true for eons, though few things are immune to change, and the story of the Pale Horse is no exception. Long ago, the stories say, a [daemon](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386) traveled the pathways between the planes, consuming the souls of the dead before they reached the Lady of Graves. He appeared as a silent white stallion, and his name was the Pale Horse. Now, they speak of a [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher who guards mortal souls while they journey into the afterlife, hunting any who dare steal from the River of Souls. His name is the Pale Horse, and he serves the Gray Lady.

The Pale Horse is the usher of duty and burden, but where once his craven whims held him captive, now he fulfills the divine charge of Pharasma, obligated to her service until the day he has saved half as many souls as he damned in a previous life. He is the Lash and the Plough, worshipped by bounty hunters, servants, beleaguered workers, risen daemons like himself, and by those who have been wronged. His wrath is legendary, though many psychopomps fear his vengeance will turn on the [Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39) once his sentence is complete.

](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

anathema: Allow your accomplishments to go unattributed, destroy another artist’s legacy, misattribute or obscure the authorship of a work of art

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Phlegyas](/Deities.aspx?ID=546)
(The Consoler of Atheists)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Before she was a [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher, Phlegyas was mortal. The most brilliant artist of her age, she was also vain and prideful, and when her arrogance led to blasphemy she was cast out from [Shelyn’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=293) church. In her excommunication, the clergy sought to strike her name from history, but they failed. Phlegyas’s legacy did more than merely survive; the artist’s name and achievements went on to last forever.

When Phlegyas came before [Pharasma](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) for judgment, the Lady of Graves saw a mortal who would defy death, the gods, or even [the multiverse](/Rules.aspx?ID=2988) itself should it dare stand in her way; who challenged and redefined her gender in life and then her fate in death; who would not compromise on showing the world the truest version of herself, forever. Pharasma saw in Phlegyas a yearning to be known fundamentally to all mortals, and so elevated her into an usher of her court.

Phlegyas became the patron of art that outlasts its creator, of the defiance of mortality, and of new parents, especially those who die in childbirth. She is the Consoler of Atheists, caring for the souls of mortal atheists and preserving long-forgotten artworks in the Anonymous Loggia, her realm within the [Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39). While Shelyn holds domain over beauty, Phlegyas represents all that transpires between the art and the viewer, and the works she inspires mortals to create are often painful and horrifying.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Saloc](/Deities.aspx?ID=547)
(The Minder of Immortals)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

_[**PFS Note**](/pathfinder-society) This deity grants [_illusory scene_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1570) instead of dreaming potential_

Ruler of the planar metropolis of Spire’s Edge in the [Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39), the [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher Saloc is [humanoid](/Traits.aspx?ID=628), but their face is devoid of features save stag horns, and two golden rings lined with eyes constantly rotate about their body. As a defense counsel in [Pharasma’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) court, the Minder of Immortals argues that a person’s intentions should be considered in equal part to the consequences of their actions when deciding their soul’s afterlife. Saloc has even been known to resurrect condemned souls to give them a second chance to prove themselves. Mortals revere Saloc as the patron of agency, bronze, and education. These followers are people who seek to improve themselves or others, including both students and teachers. Some seek to earn their freedom from earthly prisons, while others wish to change their ways to avoid punishment in the hereafter.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Ssila’meshnik](/Deities.aspx?ID=548)
(The Colorless Lord)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Ssila’meshnik holds answers and freedom for all who call on them. They are the [protean](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457) lord of fate, freedom, and paradox, and though they see the paths of all possible futures, the present is often out of reach. They exist as a flurry of shadowed afterimages, cast out across the infinite branches of time and choice. Though the Colorless Lord knows the twists and turns all lives take, those who call on them inevitably turn away from the answers the lord provides. Those who call on Ssila’meshnik always abandon their path.

They are a frequent though uninvited guest of [Pharasma’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) court, where they appear to argue the fate of whichever soul is up for judgment at that moment. Ssila’meshnik has no realm in the [Maelstrom](/Planes.aspx?ID=43), though several of their temples are scattered across the plane. The largest of their temples is in the center of Galisemni: an iridescent crystalline tower where bubbles of wild magic and ripples in time fester like extraplanar rot. The Lord of Paradox is worshipped by [ganzi](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=32) [nephilim](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=68), proteans without a chorus, and mortals better defined by their desperation than the object of their desperate yearning, and these followers honor them by living lives of constant contradiction and self-sabotage.

<column gap="tiny">

](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

Fate, freedom, paradox

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Telastmar](/Deities.aspx?ID=549)
(The Cosmic Dyne)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Whether mortals realize it or not, the stars, moons, systems, and galaxies of [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26) all flow in accordance with ancient and fundamental laws set in motion at the dawn of creation. Though magic might pull them from their charted course, even this movement is guided by a force sewn into the fabric of the cosmos. Telastmar, the Cosmic Dyne, is the manifestation of these celestial orbits, keeping the Universe in constant movement. Because this turn of planets and moons controls the tides, Telastmar also holds sway over the oceans, though they curiously seem more concerned with the fish that live in such waters. Adherents claim that Telastmar is the lord and creator of all fish, guiding them on their migrations throughout rivers and seas.

Like most [aeons](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=357), Telastmar has more concern with natural laws than mortal lives, but they can be pleased and courted by would-be adherents. Those who study the stars and planets can sometimes find divine inspiration in their elaborate orreries, as the same patterns that underpin the cosmos converge to provide glimpses of the future or fortuitous turns of fate. They also favor space travelers, and a tiny few who have returned to their home planets have claimed sightings of the great starry god-whale swimming alongside their ship.

Most of Telastmar’s worshippers are in academic centers, as the first arbitrator is too esoteric to attract followers from working fishers and sailors. Their mortal cult typically attracts astronomers and travelers who journey between the stars. Those attuned to the tides, stars, and wildlife migrations of the sea, such as migrant Taotake families or the sailors of Minata, sometimes see Telastmar, though most consider them to be a large, powerful spirit. Telastmar has also been known to shine a rare light on those who have become lost in life due to ill fate—a particularly unusual trait in an aeon.

](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

**Edicts** Aid natural migrations, chart the stars and tides, observe and calculate patterns, build orreries

**Area of Concern**
Fish, gravity, orbits, planets

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Teshallas](/Deities.aspx?ID=550)
(The Primordial Poison)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

When the Universe was young, [Pharasma](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) watched mortals as they lived, and when their day arrived, she saw mortality’s love for life push them to defy the cycle and refuse death. Pharasma raised up an asp, reshaping them into a new [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher whose venom—age— would flow through all mortal veins, draining away their memories, their passions, and their vitality, until, when their time comes to its close, they are ready for death’s embrace. 

Teshallas is the Primordial Poison, the usher of age, poison, and all venomous creatures. Though there are many who curse their name, or curse Pharasma’s cruelty for inflicting them on the world, death is not the only gift their venom bears. Their touch allows mortals to grow and mature throughout their lives, gaining the wisdom that comes only through experience. Teshallas is the patron of herbalists, and of poisons refined into medicines that give life instead of take. Their mortal following attracts hedge witches, herbalists, alchemists, and assassins, and these worshippers often maintain small gardens where they cultivate a variety of poisonous plants needed for potion making and alchemy.

Aging, poison, venomous creatures

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Vale](/Deities.aspx?ID=551)
(The Court of Ancestors)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

When souls are judged in [Pharasma’s](/Deities.aspx?ID=290) court, the [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher Vale serves as the [Boneyard’s](/Planes.aspx?ID=39) chief prosecutor. They judge mortals on their actions and the impact their actions have on the world, disregarding their thoughts, beliefs, and even intentions. To Vale, a mortal only proves themselves good or evil through deeds enacted and choices made; there is no kernel of innate purity or corruption within a mortal soul, and no change wrought upon the multiverse from the privacy of the heart or mind.

Vale was created when the first wave of the River of Souls crashed against the Boneyard, carrying the cosmic weight of all mortal cultures and the influence their cultures wrought upon their hearts. They are called the Court of Ancestors, as they are the usher of consequences, superstitions, and mortal traditions, gathering and safeguarding knowledge of life on the Universe. They also care for the souls of mortals whose faith was dedicated to concepts or philosophies unrelated to the gods. Their worshippers include storytellers and those currently mourning a great loss, and they construct temples or pray to the usher in archives, courthouses, next to gallows, and before their hearths.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Vavaalrav](/Deities.aspx?ID=552)
(The Steeple’s Skull)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Mortals have always needed ways to remember and honor their ancestors, and it was from this fundamental need the [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher Vavaalrav first imagined the spires and steeples of the [Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39). Vavaalrav is the guardian of sacred places, and the chill of his breath and stillness of his presence is felt across every mausoleum, tomb, and other burial ground of [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26). The Steeple’s Skull does not tolerate when the sanctity of a holy site or the rest of the dead is disturbed, and his symbol is used by mortals to ward graves against foul spirits and grave robbers alike.

The psychopomp’s cult attracts mortal architects, groundskeepers, mortuary workers, and masons, as well as many gargoyles. Even when they don’t worship the usher of graves, [gargoyles](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=49) whose perches adorn cathedrals, temples, and tombs pay Vavaalrav the respect he is due as patron and protector of the hallowed grounds they call home.

Vavaalrav’s home, the Wailing Tor, is an impressive spire at the center of the Boneyard’s Eight Courts. Within its walls, all souls find the peace and rest of the afterlife, and the usher takes special care to welcome and watch over spirits who struggled to accept their fate and leave their mortal lives behind.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Monitor Demigods
](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

**Edicts** Create and erect grave markers, lay bodies to rest, protect graveyards and sacred grounds from despoilment and supernatural threats

anathema: Attempt to regulate others’ emotions, dismiss or mock a creature’s suffering, surrender without a fight
edict: Aid the depressed, comfort the grieving, experience and act on intense emotions
markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Vonymos](/Deities.aspx?ID=553)
(The Mourning Storm)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Grief is a powerful emotion, felt by every mortal in every lifetime, and from the pain of intense loss crashing through mortal hearts a [psychopomp](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=458) usher was born: Vonymos, the Mourning Storm. They embody all that grows from a heart in mourning, both good and ill. They are depression, isolation, and desperation, but they are also last stands against the dark, renewed vigor, and hope. The usher’s touch is felt when emotions swell, building up until they grow too overwhelming to contain, when the walls within crumble and the flood is unleashed. They are the crack of lighting that pushes mortals through despair, igniting the inner fire needed to make a final, desperate rally before succumbing to defeat. They are the usher of catastrophes, of last stands, and of suicides.

Vonymos is worshipped by mortal bards, counselors, and crusaders, who gather in abandoned tombs and erect great circles of standing stones over mass graves. In the [Boneyard](/Planes.aspx?ID=39), the usher tends to souls of those who fall to suicide, martyrdom, and catastrophes, while their cult offers solace and comfort to those left behind on [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26).

<column gap="tiny">

](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=12)

**Edicts** Aid the depressed, comfort the grieving, experience and act on intense emotions

**Anathema** Attempt to regulate others’ emotions, dismiss or mock a creature’s suffering, surrender without a fight

**Area of Concern**
Catastrophes, last stands, suicides

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Ydajisk](/Deities.aspx?ID=554)
(Mother of Tongues)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

The [protean](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=457) lord of language evolution, lost words, and slang, Ydajisk manifests as a protean with six arms and a cobra’s hood, surrounded by a cloud of drifting sounds, symbols, shapes, and myriad other sensory experiences that shift endlessly in and out of existence. Closer inspection reveals that their body is made from interlacing strands of poetic protean script. Ydajisk is also called the Mother of Tongues, though like all proteans, their gender changes as they will. Followers of Ydajisk are wanderers, reviving dead languages from ancient ruins, chronicling the dying tongues of cultures in decline, and discovering or inventing new words. All such knowledge acquired by agents of the Mother of Tongues passes into Ydajisk’s realm, the Library of Stolen Words, where it is transcribed into books or stored in magical containers, alongside scrolls and tomes considered long lost by mortal scholars.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Grask Uldeth](/Deities.aspx?ID=555)
(The Uniter)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Few mortals know that Grask was killed by assassins hired by his own faithful majordomo, Ardax the White Hair; fewer still understand that neither party sees this as a betrayal. Grask keenly sought a violent death specifically to swear his Deathright, while Ardax’s unusually prophetic powers of foresight saw a future where Grask would swear the [orcs](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=66) to Tar-Baphon when the Whispering Tyrant returned. After being assassinated, Grask challenged the god Zagresh and won, becoming a god. 

Almost immediately after his ascension, Grask attempted to assassinate the [dwarf](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=59) god [Torag](/Deities.aspx?ID=294), driving an axe forged by dwarven hands into the older deity’s chest. It is entirely possible that Grask would have succeeded, spurring a new era of violence and war between dwarves and orcs, were it not for the intervention of the new orc god [Uirch](/Deities.aspx?ID=560), who saved Torag’s life and drove Grask from the field of battle in a fearsome duel.

In life, Grask’s vision was to amass wealth via trade rather than raiding, and he imposed a semblance of law and order to entice foreign merchants to visit Belkzen. At first, this wasn’t a popular concept among a population that largely gained wealth via raids, but that changed when the gold started rolling in.

As a deity, the Uniter values those who strictly adhere to the rules and examples he set down in life. He is open to trade and negotiation with other ancestries, though he steadfastly refuses to forgive dwarves for the crimes he believes they have committed against his people. He expects total obedience from the rank and file and that those in power rule with an iron fist, all in the name of improving the orcs’ lot in life. He is, in many ways, the mirror of Uirch, who believes that power should be used to protect those weaker than oneself.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Orc Gods](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=29)

**Edicts** Improve the orcs’ lot in life, obey the orcs above your station, rule the orcs below you with a strong hand

**Anathema** Allow orc-kind to become weak, intentionally make orcs look bad, disrupt your hierarchy without just cause

**Area of Concern**
Discipline, power, prosperity

</column>

edict: Work together, push each other to greater heights, respect the animals you hunt
markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Jukha](/Deities.aspx?ID=556)
(The Big Game Huntress)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Jukha is the goddess of big game hunting. She favors those who work together to bring down large beasts to feed their village, especially if they do so under the cover of night.

As a mortal, Jukha was a [dromaar](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=70) with dreams similar to [Grask](/Deities.aspx?ID=555): she wanted to lead orcs into a new age of prosperity by changing how their neighboring countries viewed them. She planned to end the raids, unite the holds of Belkzen, and kill Tar-Baphon to prove her intentions. Despite being a teenager, Jukha gained a following through sheer physical prowess and ferocity, but she failed to inspire teamwork in her followers, and her group splintered. In frustration, she went on a solo hunt for a [mammoth](/Monsters.aspx?ID=2994), but she and her prey simultaneously landed lethal blows. She and the mammoth breathed their last together and before she died, Jukha whispered her Deathright.

Upon her ascension, she became the Relentless Huntress, goddess of big game hunting, out of spite. She didn’t make her presence known to mortals for some time, however, choosing to ponder her life. Over time, she realized her mistakes and decided to pass these lessons to mortals. She realized that big game hunting was the perfect way for her to share the necessity of working together.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Orc Gods](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=29)

**Edicts** Work together, push each other to greater heights, respect the animals you hunt

**Anathema** Abandon or betray your people, refuse to learn from your mistakes, isolate yourself

**Area of Concern**
Communal hunting, evening, feasts

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Mahja Firehair](/Deities.aspx?ID=557)
(Matron of Rites)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

As a mortal, Mahja Firehair was a follower of [Sarenrae](/Deities.aspx?ID=292) and the warlord of the Burning Sun hold, which was initially made up of outcasts from other holds. Through Mahja’s heroic and fearsome reputation, she oversaw the rapid growth and expansion of her people. After her death, she defeated the reigning orc god of fire, Sezelrian, in the Crucible and became a deity. Mahja carries much of Sarenrae’s teachings into her own values as a goddess and shares Sarenrae’s affinity for fire, but her primary concern is teaching her followers to bring glory to all orcs and to instill a sense of willing sacrifice into her followers if that is what’s required to achieve this goal.

Much like Sarenrae, Mahja values fire’s literal and figurative cleansing. However, she believes redemption should be earned, rather than given freely. In fact, Mahja strongly believes in proving one’s worth in all aspects of life. She routinely tested people before her ascension and was known to ask leading questions and request demonstrations of people’s abilities before she took them seriously. She also commonly gave people difficult tasks before considering trades, deals, or alliances. However, she was fiercely loyal to those who proved their worth to her.

The Matron of Rites has quickly established her place as a deity among the orc people, though she’s not a goddess for the complacent. Her followers must prove what they can offer her cause, and she scorns those who make empty promises.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Orc Gods](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=29)

**Edicts** Bring honor and glory to orcs, build up your community, seek personal perfection

**Anathema** Undermine your community, seek personal glory, grant redemption to the unworthy

Community, fire, trials of worth

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Nulgreth](/Deities.aspx?ID=558)
(The Blood God)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Nulgreth is one of the oldest orc deities, and is thought to be the origin of the ferocity that allows [orcs](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=66) to lash out in battle even when slain. If nothing else, he’s certainly a prime example of that ferocity due to his constant rage and endless bloodlust. One need not worry about him scheming or plotting, just the sharp edge of his axe. 

There have been many throughout history who enjoy battle and killing, but the Blood God revels in both the killing and the blood that follows. He drenches his blood-red skin in the blood and entrails of his fallen foes and expects the same from his followers as he directs them to fight all over Golarion. Most of his followers’ ceremonies and celebrations involve blood in some way or another, often after the sacrifice of a prisoner. Nulgreth’s worshippers also like to paint their skin red with blood or dyes.

Though Nulgreth has great skill in battle, other orc deities can find it difficult to work with him, as he bores easily and prefers to solve all problems through violence.

<column gap="tiny">

Blood, rage, strength

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Rull](/Deities.aspx?ID=559)
(The Thunderer)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

Shortly after [orcs](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=66) arrived on the surface of [Golarion](/Rules.aspx?ID=3298), it’s said that Rull challenged thunder to a fight. He climbed a mountain peak during a thunderstorm and yelled out his Deathright to a spirit of lightning. He was struck by a tremendous bolt of lightning on the spot and died. During the ensuing Crucible, Rull claims to have won by consuming the lightning spirit he had challenged, and that it now lives within him and is under his control. 

Since Rull’s reign began, lighting strikes in Belkzen haven’t been seen as an act of nature, but either a sign that Rull is unhappy with something or that he’s granting a favor to a follower. As such, the Thunderer’s followers pray to him to bring lightning upon their foes instead of themselves. Similarly, clear weather is seen as a sign of Rull’s disapproval. In times of blue skies, orcs in Belkzen often perform rituals involving copious noise and drumming to bring back the clouds, which block out the painful sun. For a follower of Rull, clouds and rainstorms are the ideal weather.

**Area of Concern**

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Uirch](/Deities.aspx?ID=560)
(The Bulwark)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 316

In life, Uirch was a follower of [Sarenrae](/Deities.aspx?ID=292) and a warpriest in the Burning Sun hold under warlord Mahja. He became a god upon usurping the tyrannical god Lanishra. Like [Mahja](/Deities.aspx?ID=557), Uirch has incorporated much of Sarenrae’s teachings into his own, but his primary goals are to protect those who cannot protect themselves and prevent tyranny from taking root. Prior to his ascension, Uirch spent much of his time learning various ways to protect people, both physically and mentally. He also studied the most impactful preventive measures to ensure people wouldn’t need protection in the first place. One such measure that Uirch strongly advocates is inhibiting tyranny from taking hold. He encourages leaders to be strong and benevolent, but also to ensure that there exists a system to keep a watchful eye out for those in power who might exceed the duties of their station and become tyrants. He instructs all his followers to do the same, and there are already several small orc holds adopting his philosophies and placing his priests in positions as guides and counselors.

Now, Uirch the Bulwark spends much of his time passing on knowledge to his followers, both directly and indirectly. As a newly ascended god, he has not yet created a strongly codified system of rules and guidelines. He asks that his followers stamp out abuses of power, protect the innocent, and ensure that they remain ready and capable of facing whatever trials and dangers might assail them or those who turn to them for protection.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Orc Gods](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=29)

**Anathema** Allow tyranny to stand uncontested, put your own safety before those in need of defending, make a promise with the intent to break it

**Area of Concern**
Fortifications, leadership, protection

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Varg](/Deities.aspx?ID=561)
(The Iron Warrior)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Varg is another of the older orc deities and is said to be the first [orc](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=66) to learn how to forge blades. In life, Varg was a notably intelligent orc who focused exclusively on waging war. He recognized that the orcs’ enemies were better equipped, better trained, and used better tactics in battle. He sought to learn these things, which allowed him to become a prestigious warlord. However, Varg’s experience in combat was against other orcs. His overconfidence led him to lay “siege” to a walled city outside Belkzen. The siege didn’t last long. In fact, Varg died quickly and his hold scattered.

Before every battle, Varg was known to declare his Deathright. When he succeeded at his Crucible, he spent a great deal of time studying mortals and how they waged war. Siege engines caught his attention, and he gave the secrets of building them to his mortal brethren. Orcs often use these weapons of war to pay homage to the Iron Warrior, but a number see the use of technology for anything other than combat as blasphemy.

<column gap="tiny">

**Category** [Orc Gods](/DeityCategories.aspx?ID=29)

**Edicts** Use tactics rather than relying solely on brute force or overwhelming numbers, use siege engines for combat whenever possible, seek out new technology

**Anathema** Pursue technology for non-combat purposes, use machinery and other advanced technology outside of combat, keep advanced weaponry or battle tactics a secret from orcs

**Area of Concern**
Innovation, iron, tactics, technology, war

</column>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Standard">
[Wulgren](/Deities.aspx?ID=562)
(The Bone Breaker)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Wulgren, like [Jukha](/Deities.aspx?ID=556), is a [dromaar](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=70). He traveled exceptionally far and wide as a mortal before his ascension. Wulgren was born in Belkzen but became separated from his party during a scouting expedition. After a brief period held captive by a group of adventuring Pathfinders, Wulgren was freed on his own recognizance and traveled to Alkenstar, the City of Smog.

While in Alkenstar, Wulgren developed a great skill in weapon smithing and forged an unlikely and powerful [firearm](/WeaponGroups.aspx?ID=16): the [barricade buster](/Weapons.aspx?ID=330). With this massive, inaccurate, but deadly weapon in hand, Wulgren swore his Deathright and returned to Belkzen to stand against the hordes of the Whispering Tyrant. During a brutal battle against an endless stream of skeletal undead, Wulgren and his mighty weapon ultimately fell, and he continued on to fight his Crucible.

It’s unclear which orc deity Wulgren slew; most likely it was another orc who had passed the Crucible previously, whose name had not yet spread widely enough to be well known. Whoever they were, Wulgren defeated them (though it’s unclear if he did so with his barricade buster or more conventional weaponry), and Wulgren the Bone-Breaker now stands proudly among Belkzen’s gods.

(The Primal Chaos)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Azathoth is the [Daemon](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=386) Sultan and the Primal Chaos, a roiling mass of destructive and transformative power the size of a sun, dwelling in the darkness between the stars deep in the center of the universe. There, masked from mortal sight by a veil of swirling colors, he is surrounded by the other Outer Gods that make up his court, dancing and cavorting about him endlessly, filling the void with the sound of ghastly flutes. Azathoth is utterly unaware of and uncaring toward those few who have come to revere and worship him. It is precisely this blind, uncaring nature that makes Azathoth the perfect embodiment of a blind, uncaring universe. Azathoth’s name, however, has great power over the Outer Gods when properly invoked. He has also sometimes been summoned by mortal priests— and though these summons attract only a tiny sliver of his attention and manifest as a form other than that of the Primal Chaos, they nevertheless lead to destruction on a massive scale.

In his cosmic writhing, Azathoth sometimes throws off seeds that travel through the cosmos as malignant comets or meteors, until they finally crash into a planet. There, they slowly gestate and spawn, bringing cataclysmic ruin to the world. Azathoth is also associated with the bizarre reptilian birds known as shantaks; when a mortal foolishly tries to make a shantak their mount but lacks the strength of will to maintain control over the creature, the shantak flies into the depths of space through means impossible to quantify, bringing its hapless passenger to Azathoth’s court for one final glimpse into the horrifying truth of the universe.

<column gap="tiny">

**Anathema** None

**Area of Concern**
Entropy, madness, mindless destruction

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Bokrug](/Deities.aspx?ID=568)
(The Water Lizard)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Silently dwelling beneath the surface of a nameless lake within a far-flung corner of the [Dreamlands](/Planes.aspx?ID=47), Bokrug was once the patron deity of an ancestry known only as the beings of Ib—amphibious humanoids with froglike features—but the incursion of humans upon their domain resulted in the total extermination of this shoreside civilization. Shades of these creatures still haunt that area. Bokrug’s preferred form is of a colossal green [lizard](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=440) whose back is covered in needle-like spines, possessing a long tail which forks into a trio of spikes at its tip and a noxious breath that induces hysteria. Despite this fearsome appearance, the Water Lizard is rarely moved to aggression; however, when his ire is drawn he ceaselessly pursues those who transgressed against him in a violent fury, returning to a docile state only when his prey is utterly destroyed.

Worship of the Water Lizard is primarily observed among residents of waterside or swampy settlements who hope that by placating the god their home will be spared his wrath. Storms over the water are interpreted as signs of Bokrug’s displeasure, whereas extended periods of calm weather are his most common boon.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Cthulhu](/Deities.aspx?ID=569)
(The Dreamer in the Deep)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Slumbering in drowned R’lyeh, the Dreamer in the Deep is a towering monstrosity whose many tentacles reach out beyond the physical, influencing the minds of sapient beings across the Universe through their dreams. Artists and visionaries, whose creativity makes them especially susceptible to Cthulhu’s influence, may wake to find themselves unconsciously inspired by memories they cannot fully grasp. As a result, countless works of art and philosophy have been produced by mortals that touch upon aspects of this Great Old One, each subtly lengthening the god’s reach.

Small cults of Cthulhu gather in secret throughout [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), with the majority of their membership consisting of those who dwell within the ocean or along its coasts, as well as small contingents within decadent cities. Cthulhu’s cultists prophesize that one day, given the proper alignment of the stars, the Dreamer shall awaken, scouring entire planets in preparation for the return of his dread kin.

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Hastur](/Deities.aspx?ID=570)
(The King in Yellow)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Hastur is a Great Old One who, though confined to the city of Carcosa on a planet orbiting a faraway star, is nevertheless in the midst of a transformation into a true Outer God. He works toward this feat through his avatar, the King in Yellow, which can manifest anywhere in the universe the light from Carcosa’s sun can be seen. The King in Yellow appears as a humanoid figure draped in yellow clothing, but there is nothing within the cloth save a shapeless, horrifying presence. Hastur is also referred to as Him Who Is Not To Be Named, or simply the Unspeakable.

Self-indulgence and nihilism are at the heart of Hastur’s worship, and he is popular among debauched artists and nobles who recognize no purpose to life other than gratifying their own increasingly grotesque and outlandish tastes. Cults of Hastur are united in their use of the Yellow Sign, a triskelion design that allows Hastur to observe and possess those who gaze upon it. The presence of the Yellow Sign among a city’s graffiti is a foreboding indication that Hastur’s attention has been drawn there, and these settlements soon find themselves under attack either by Hastur himself—who seeks to draw cities into Carcosa, fueling his transformation into an Outer God—or by Xhamen-Dor, a parasitic deity spawned by Hastur as he walked Carcosa’s sewers.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Mhar](/Deities.aspx?ID=571)
(The World Thunder)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

Imprisoned under the colossal peak of Mhar Massif following a botched attempt to burrow into [the Universe](/Planes.aspx?ID=26), the World Thunder is a mysterious Great Old One commanding the elements of earth and fire. Though the primordial flames of Golarion’s core suspend the god in a sleep-like trance, its cult believes that tectonic activity—both natural events and those created by Mhar’s more powerful priests— will one day emancipate their destructive master, ushering in an age of fire and ash that will consume [Golarion](/Rules.aspx?ID=3298). Icons created in veneration of Mhar depict a towering mountain of jagged stone, with great falls of lava pouring from its ashwreathed summit and clawed crystalline limbs reaching out in every direction from its base. Whether this is Mhar’s true form remains a mystery to mortals, however, as every world Mhar has previously manifested within was invariably destroyed once the heat of its core could no longer keep it from stirring.

<title level="1" right="Deity " pfs="Restricted">
[Nhimbaloth](/Deities.aspx?ID=572)
(The Empty Death)
</title>

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">

<traits>

</traits>

**Source** [Divine Mysteries](/Sources.aspx?ID=234) pg. 318

An especially reclusive Outer God who hunts along the shores of the River of Souls, Nhimbaloth is a shapeless entity known as the Empty Death. She preys on those who hunt souls as they travel down the river, but she devours both hunter and soul indiscriminately; those she consumes have no afterlife or potential for resurrection. They are forever gone, and forever nothing.

Nhimbaloth is said to see through [will-o’-wisps](/MonsterFamilies.aspx?ID=210), and her trace is left behind in a symmetrical pattern of seven divots along the shoreline, said to be her fingerprints. Faceless [undead](/MonsterTemplates.aspx?ID=3) haunt the places she has passed through, and plant and animal life in the area is especially hostile to the living. One place where she has particular influence is within the Mushfens of southern Varisia, where will-o’- wisp oracles sap the drive and reason from their victims and leave them wandering in a vacuous stupor through the blasted swamp.
